pattern: Since taking the current largest value is never worse than taking any smaller one, and shrinking a value can only ever reduce future gains, a greedy repeated-max strategy driven by a heap is always at least as good as any other order of operations.
hints: At every step you want to grab the single largest value available right now — using a smaller one instead never helps later, since the shrink rule only ever makes values smaller or equal.
Repeatedly scanning the whole array for the current maximum works but is slow; a max-heap lets you fetch and update the largest value in logarithmic time.
Pop the maximum, add it to the running score, replace it with its ceiling-divided-by-three value, and push that back onto the heap; repeat this exactly k times and return the accumulated score.

edge_inputs.description: identical values, one operation applied to each
a single element that repeatedly gets divided down
only one operation is allowed, so just the single largest value is taken
the value one never shrinks further, since ceil(1/3) is still 1
one dominant value must be picked repeatedly until it falls below the others
operations far outnumber the array length, forcing many repeats over a tiny array
a value near the top of the allowed range combined with a small operation count
several elements tie for the current maximum

stress.description: randomized arrays and operation counts, bounded so the brute-force oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    bound = max(1, min(size, 60))
    n = rng.randint(1, bound)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, bound)
    return [nums, k]

solutions.python:
import heapq

class Solution:
    def maxKelements(self, nums, k):
        heap = [-x for x in nums]
        heapq.heapify(heap)
        score = 0
        for _ in range(k):
            x = -heapq.heappop(heap)
            score += x
            nxt = -(-x // 3)
            heapq.heappush(heap, -nxt)
        return score
